diagrams.title: Shared Memory Model
diagrams.description: Shows two processes (Process A and Process B) connected to a shared memory segment. This illustrates how processes can exchange data by reading and writing to a common memory region, which is one of the fastest forms of IPC but requires synchronization mechanisms like semaphores.
elements: Shared Memory Segment (center, connecting both processes)
Arrows showing read/write operations from processes to shared memory
Process labels and memory address labels
diagrams.purpose: Explains the shared memory IPC mechanism where processes directly access common memory, highlighting the need for synchronization to prevent race conditions.

diagrams.title: Message Passing Model
diagrams.description: Depicts two processes communicating through a message queue or direct message passing. Shows a sender process sending a message through a communication channel to a receiver process, with the OS kernel managing the message queue in kernel space.
elements: Message envelopes traveling through the channel
Send and Receive system call indicators
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates how processes exchange messages via a communication channel managed by the operating system, demonstrating asynchronous communication with buffering.

diagrams.title: Pipe Communication
diagrams.description: Shows a parent process creating a pipe and forking a child process to communicate. The pipe appears as a unidirectional channel where the parent writes to the write-end and the child reads from the read-end.
elements: Read end and Write end labels
File descriptor indicators (fd[0] for read, fd[1] for write)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the pipe IPC mechanism, a unidirectional communication channel between related processes (parent-child), showing how file descriptors enable communication.
